event_description: Boeing formally launches 737 MAX program as re-engine of existing 737 platform; decision driven by American Airlines threat to purchase Airbus A320neo; internal Boeing engineering preference for clean-sheet new aircraft overridden by competitive and financial pressure; re-engine locks certification approach to existing type rating framework before design begins

American Airlines contract signed requiring 737 MAX to maintain commonality with existing 737 type certificate; contract explicitly conditions order on no new type rating requirement for pilots; financial commitment made to 460 aircraft; contractual constraint locks training and certification approach before engineering design is complete

Boeing reports record backlog of 737 orders; investor communications emphasize MAX program as primary growth driver; production rate targets set at 47/month by 2017; financial forecasts publicly committed to Wall Street; schedule pressure now has external investor accountability layer compounding internal program pressure

FAA grants Boeing expanded Organization Designation Authorization; Boeing employees authorized to certify Boeing aircraft on behalf of FAA; FAA oversight role structurally reduced; Boeing ODA employees report to Boeing management with divided accountability to FAA; external correction mechanism compromised before MCAS designed

MCAS introduced into 737 MAX design to compensate for aerodynamic pitch-up tendency caused by repositioned larger LEAP engines; system designed to automatically push nose down when angle-of-attack sensor detects high AoA; single sensor input architecture chosen; no redundancy at design stage; system authority set at 0.6 degrees stabilizer movement
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: MCAS authority expanded in design from 0.6 degrees to 2.5 degrees stabilizer movement per activation; change made to address handling qualities at higher AoA than originally modeled; expanded authority not reflected in updated safety assessment; safety analysis document continues to reference original 0.6 degree assumption; discrepancy not resolved
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: Boeing System Safety Analysis classifies MCAS failure as hazardous rather than catastrophic; classification based on assumption pilots would recognize and counteract erroneous MCAS activation within 3 seconds using existing runaway stabilizer procedure; analysis does not account for repeated activations; does not account for crews unaware of MCAS existence; classification determines level of FAA oversight required

Decision made to omit MCAS entirely from Flight Crew Operations Manual; pilots will not be informed of system existence, authority, or behavior; decision explicitly linked to maintaining no-new-type-rating commitment to airlines; Boeing manager emails reference keeping MAX training package to iPad level; omission means pilots have no procedure for MCAS-specific failure

Internal Boeing emails surface showing employees describing certification pressure; one employee writes that 737 MAX is designed by clowns supervised by monkeys; separate employee expresses concern about MCAS behavior in simulator; concerns shared within working group but not escalated to safety review board or certification team; pattern of internal signal containment begins

FAA issues revised ODA guidance expanding scope of Boeing self-certification authority; FAA safety engineers report being excluded from key design reviews; Boeing ODA employees report management pressure when safety findings conflict with schedule milestones; one ODA employee documents being removed from program after raising concerns; accountability coupling between certification finding and correction action degraded
source_name: FAA Office of Inspector General Report 2019

event_description: Boeing projects 737 MAX production rate of 47 aircraft per month by 2017; delivery schedule committed to airline customers with financial penalty clauses for delay; investor guidance quantifies MAX contribution to earnings per share; program now has three simultaneous external commitments — airline contracts, investor guidance, FAA type certificate timeline — all requiring schedule maintenance

Internal Boeing engineering review recommends dual AoA sensor input for MCAS redundancy; single sensor architecture means single point of failure drives entire stabilizer control system; recommendation not implemented; cost and weight cited; schedule impact cited; finding not escalated beyond working group; single sensor architecture locked into production design
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: AoA Disagree Alert — cockpit warning that would indicate when left and right angle-of-attack sensors give conflicting readings — designed and built but designated optional paid feature; standard aircraft configuration does not include active alert; airlines must purchase separately; alert would warn pilots of faulty AoA input driving MCAS; made optional to avoid cost impact on base aircraft price
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: FAA issues type certificate for 737 MAX; certification documentation does not accurately describe MCAS operational authority range; MCAS not listed in pilot training requirements; FAA certification engineers working under ODA delegation do not have independent visibility into final MCAS design parameters; certificate issued based on safety analysis referencing superseded MCAS authority figures
source_name: FAA Type Certificate Data Sheet A16WE

event_description: First 737 MAX delivery to Malindo Air; production ramp accelerates toward 47/month target; Boeing reports 4700 plus orders on record; investors briefed on MAX contribution to financial performance; all financial and delivery metrics positive; no public disclosure of MCAS omission from pilot training; aircraft enters commercial service with pilots unaware of system that controls stabilizer

Boeing and FAA jointly determine that AoA Disagree Alert is non-functional on majority of delivered MAX aircraft due to software configuration issue with optional Display Unit; alert fails to activate when AoA sensors disagree; Boeing and FAA discuss remediation; decision made to address in future software update rather than immediate airworthiness directive; aircraft continue operating with non-functional safety alert
source_name: FAA Office of Inspector General Report 2019

event_description: Lion Air maintenance records document AoA sensor malfunction on multiple flights preceding fatal crash; sensor replaced after JT043 flight on October 28 2018 shows erroneous readings; aircraft returned to service without investigation into whether MCAS was activated by faulty sensor; no notification issued to other airlines operating MAX; no escalation to Boeing or FAA
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: Internal Boeing communications document employee concerns about MCAS behavior during simulator evaluation; employees describe MCAS activating unexpectedly; concerns documented in internal communications but not routed to certification or flight safety review; employees describe organizational pressure not to raise issues affecting type rating determination or schedule

Lion Air Flight JT610 crashes into Java Sea 13 minutes after takeoff from Jakarta; all 189 people aboard killed; flight data recorder shows MCAS activated 26 times in final flight driven by erroneous AoA sensor reading; crew executed runaway stabilizer procedure but MCAS reactivated each time trim was released; crew had no knowledge of MCAS existence; AoA Disagree Alert non-functional on this aircraft
source_name: NTSB Accident Report AAR-22/01 Lion Air

event_description: Boeing issues Operations Manual Bulletin to all 737 MAX operators describing runaway stabilizer procedure; bulletin does not mention MCAS by name; does not disclose MCAS as cause of Lion Air crash; does not disclose single AoA sensor architecture; FAA issues Emergency Airworthiness Directive same day consistent with Boeing framing; Max continues worldwide commercial operation; 346 additional people will fly MAX before next crash

Boeing internal communications show company aware MCAS is root cause of Lion Air crash and that software update is required; Boeing internally targets MCAS software update for April 2019; senior Boeing leadership briefed on fix timeline; Max continues operating globally during eight month software development period; financial cost of grounding estimated internally and factored into decision not to ground

Boeing lobbies FAA not to require simulator training as part of MCAS software update; internal communications show Boeing calculates cost to airlines of simulator training requirement at $1M per pilot; Boeing frames simulator training as unnecessary given software fix; FAA defers to Boeing framing; commitment to no-new-type-rating maintained even after fatal crash confirmed MCAS as cause

FAA issues Airworthiness Directive requiring pilot awareness training on stabilizer runaway procedure; directive does not require simulator training; does not disclose MCAS; Boeing type rating commitment preserved post-crash; training requirement limited to iPad-based bulletin review; pilots continue operating MAX without MCAS knowledge or simulator-validated recovery procedure

Ethiopian Airlines Flight ET302 crashes six minutes after takeoff from Addis Ababa; all 157 people aboard killed; flight data recorder shows identical MCAS activation pattern to Lion Air; crew executed Boeing post-Lion Air bulletin procedure exactly as written; procedure insufficient to arrest MCAS; crew had no knowledge of MCAS; 346 total deaths across two crashes separated by 5 months
source_name: NTSB Accident Report DCA19RA107 Ethiopian Airlines

event_description: FAA orders global grounding of 737 MAX; FAA last major aviation authority to act after China Civil Aviation Administration, European Aviation Safety Agency, Transport Canada all grounded MAX in preceding 48 hours; Boeing stock falls 12 percent; production suspended; estimated 400 MAX aircraft grounded worldwide; grounding reveals FAA had not independently verified MCAS parameters before type certificate issued
source_name: FAA Emergency Order of Prohibition March 13 2019

event_description: House Transportation Committee and Senate Commerce Committee launch parallel investigations; congressional subpoenas issued for Boeing internal communications; documents show Boeing employees raised MCAS concerns that were not acted upon; ODA self-certification structure identified as systemic failure; Boeing CEO Muilenburg testifies before both committees; internal emails describing clowns and monkeys entered into congressional record

Boeing acknowledges MCAS software update will include dual AoA sensor input — the redundancy that internal engineers recommended in 2015 and was not implemented; fix that was available at design stage implemented only after 346 deaths; Boeing estimates $5B charge against earnings for MAX program costs; further charges to follow; total program cost eventually exceeds $20B

Boeing CEO Dennis Muilenburg fired; House Transportation Committee releases 238-page final report; report documents Boeing concealed MCAS information from FAA; finds FAA oversight delegation created structural conditions for failure; finds schedule and financial pressure systematically overrode safety culture; report finds Boeing employees who raised concerns were marginalized; Max remains grounded; accountability reaches executive level 14 months after first crash

FAA ungrounds 737 MAX after 20-month grounding; MCAS software update certified; dual AoA sensor input now required; pilot simulator training now required — the requirement Boeing lobbied against post-Lion Air; Max returns to service with the redundancy and training that engineers recommended before first flight; Senate Commerce Committee releases separate 199-page report confirming House findings and adding additional evidence of FAA regulatory capture

Boeing enters deferred prosecution agreement with DOJ on single count of conspiracy to defraud the United States; $2.5B settlement includes $243M criminal fine, $1.77B to airline customers, $500M to crash victims families; agreement acknowledges Boeing employees deceived FAA; no individual Boeing employees criminally charged; accountability coupling at individual level severs at settlement
